idiom: To bark up the wrong tree
meaning_english: To pursue a mistaken line of thought or course of action. [cite: 1]
To raise a false alarm. [cite: 2]
Having to start all over again because a previous attempt failed. [cite: 1]
To suffer unpleasant results of an action, especially by meddling in something. [cite: 2]

idiom: To cut a long story short
meaning_english: Used to end a long explanation and get to the main point. [cite: 1]

idiom: To bite off more than one can chew
meaning_english: To take on a task that is way too big. [cite: 2]

idiom: To let the cat out of the bag
meaning_english: To reveal a secret carelessly or by mistake. [cite: 1]
To praise oneself or one's own achievements. [cite: 2]

meaning_kannada: ಸಣ್ಣ ವಿಷಯಕ್ಕೆ ದೊಡ್ಡ ಜಗಳ ಅಥವಾ ಗೊಂದಲ
meaning_english: Great excitement or anger about a trivial matter. [cite: 1]
To use every possible course of action to achieve something. [cite: 2]
To admit that you were wrong and say you are sorry. [cite: 1]
To accept the unpleasant consequences of one's actions. [cite: 2]
To become difficult to control. [cite: 1]
To go to bed. [cite: 2]
To avoid being very close to or friendly with someone. [cite: 1]
To join others in doing something that is fashionable or likely to be successful. [cite: 2]

idiom: To keep the wolf from the door
meaning_english: To have enough money to avert hunger or starvation. [cite: 1]
To earn just enough money to live on. [cite: 2]
To take a subordinate or lesser role to someone else. [cite: 1]
To suspect that something is wrong or that someone is being dishonest. [cite: 2]
